A01.title: Broken Access Control
A01.example_attack: A user modifies an identifier in the URL or API and gains access to another user's order, report, or admin functionality without server-side role verification.
A01.how_to_find: Test horizontal and vertical access to objects (IDOR), client-side restriction bypass, and missing server-side checks for every action.
A01.how_to_fix: Enforce server-side authorization on every request, implement role-based and resource-ownership policies, and never rely solely on 'hidden' URLs or parameters.

A02.title: Security Misconfiguration
A02.example_attack: Debug pages are exposed, default credentials remain active, unnecessary HTTP methods or headers are enabled; version and internal path information leaks through error responses.
A02.how_to_find: Scan headers, TLS configuration, directory listings, and default paths; compare against hardening guides; review cloud/IaC settings and environment variables.
A02.how_to_fix: Minimize attack surface: disable unnecessary features, enforce strong TLS cipher suites, maintain a unified secure baseline, and automate configuration checks in CI/CD.

A03.title: Software Supply Chain Failures
A03.example_attack: A dependency or build pipeline is compromised: package substitution, malicious artifact in a registry, or a leaked CI secret.
A03.how_to_find: Inventory dependencies (SBOM), verify artifact integrity and provenance, audit publish permissions, and check image and commit signatures.
A03.how_to_fix: Pin dependency versions, use verified publishers, sign artifacts and verify checksums, isolate builds, grant minimal CI permissions, and monitor dependencies for known vulnerabilities.

A04.title: Cryptographic Failures
A04.example_attack: Secrets and tokens are transmitted over HTTP, weak or deprecated algorithms are used, passwords are stored without proper hashing, and IVs/keys are predictable.
A04.how_to_find: Review transport channels (TLS), secret storage locations, algorithms and key lengths; search for secrets in logs and client-side code.
A04.how_to_fix: Enforce TLS for all sensitive data, use modern AEAD ciphers, manage keys via KMS/Vault, hash passwords with bcrypt/Argon2, and avoid custom cryptographic implementations.

A05.title: Injection
A05.example_attack: SQL injection via a search form, XSS in reflected output, command injection through a shell call, or LDAP/XML injection in integrations.
A05.how_to_find: Fuzz input points, statically analyze query concatenation, review ORM/parameterization usage, and check for DOM and stored XSS in templates.
A05.how_to_fix: Use parameterized queries, apply context-aware escaping, deploy CSP where appropriate, prohibit unsafe APIs (eval, raw shell), and validate input against allowlists.

A06.title: Insecure Design
A06.example_attack: Features are designed without a threat model: trust is placed in the client, rate limits are absent, and 'temporary' authentication bypasses persist in production.
A06.how_to_find: Perform threat modeling for key scenarios, review security requirements, and identify 'security as afterthought' anti-patterns in API design and data flows.
A06.how_to_fix: Adopt a Secure SDLC, define explicit controls at the design stage, separate trust zones, enforce limits and quotas, and design according to the principle of least privilege.

A07.title: Authentication Failures
A07.example_attack: Password brute-force without lockout, weak account recovery, session fixation, token theft from localStorage, or missing MFA for sensitive operations.
A07.how_to_find: Test password policies, session management, MFA, token expiry and invalidation; verify protection against automated login attempts.
A07.how_to_fix: Implement MFA where needed, use secure session storage (HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite), enforce rate limiting, monitor anomalous logins, and adopt modern OAuth/OIDC flows.

A08.title: Software or Data Integrity Failures
A08.example_attack: Insecure deserialization, plugin or config substitution, missing update signature verification, or CI pipeline without artifact tampering protection.
A08.how_to_find: Search for insecure deserialization, unsigned updates, uploads without type and integrity validation, and inadequate permissions on critical files.
A08.how_to_fix: Sign artifacts and verify integrity, use safe serialization formats, prohibit untrusted deserialization, and enforce integrity controls in the pipeline and on hosts.

A09.title: Security Logging & Alerting Failures
A09.example_attack: An attacker erases traces or actions go unlogged; administrators remain unaware of a compromise due to noise or lack of correlation.
A09.how_to_find: Verify audit coverage for authentication, admin actions, and data changes; confirm log tamper protection; check for alerts on critical events.
A09.how_to_fix: Use structured logs without secrets, centralize collection, store logs immutably, and maintain runbooks and alerts keyed to key compromise indicators.

A10.title: Mishandling of Exceptional Conditions
A10.example_attack: Service crashes from an unhandled exception, internal details leak in error responses, or denial of service through edge-case input.
A10.how_to_find: Run load and negative tests, review error handling and timeouts, and verify that stack traces and internal paths are never returned to the client.
A10.how_to_fix: Return a uniform, safe error format to clients, log details only server-side, enforce resource limits, implement circuit breakers, and set correct status codes and timeouts for dependencies.
